petitioner_background: The Petitioner is a writer and lecturer about the game of poker who sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). He earned doctorates in mathematics and mathematical logic from a university and has applied his mathematical knowledge to poker. He writes about poker for various websites and teaches a class at an institution offering classes to students aged 50 and older. At the time of filing in July 2019, he was working on a book that may be accompanied by a video series.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted printouts of two articles from late 2018 that appeared on several websites, including websites of local affiliates of major television networks (CBS, ABC, FOX, NBC). The wording of each version was identical, indicating a common source, and the articles were credited to 'Marketers Media,' identified as a 'Press Release Distribution Service.' Many printouts included a disclaimer stating the content was provided by an independent third-party content provider and that Frankly (the owner of the affiliate) and the station made no warranties. The record also included a 'Press Release Distribution Report' for one of the articles. Translated copies of foreign-language articles about the Petitioner were also submitted but lacked author credits. Additional articles included one in a student-run publication at an institution (noted by a blog post from the institution), and articles on the websites of Pokerground and a Reporter site, each receiving less than 40,000 visits per month.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the articles credited to 'Marketers Media' were commissioned press releases rather than independent journalism. The Petitioner's possession of a Press Release Distribution Report supported that he or someone on his behalf engaged a marketing company to issue press releases to media outlets that claimed no responsibility for the contents. Third-party content published with such disclaimers cannot be considered equivalent to news coverage and is more akin to advertising than journalism. The foreign-language articles lacked required author credits, greatly restricting probative value especially in conjunction with evidence that the Petitioner essentially purchased media coverage. The student-run publication did not show significant readership beyond the institution's students, employees, and alumni. The Pokerground and Reporter sites with less than 40,000 monthly visits did not constitute major media, in contrast to local television affiliate sites that attract hundreds of thousands or millions. The Petitioner did not establish these constituted professional or major trade publications or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted letters from officials of Winning Poker Network (WPN), Upswing Poker, and PokerNews stating that the Petitioner makes valuable contributions as a columnist for the websites, and that he suggested important revisions to WPN's system. Counsel asserted that 'Upswing Poker is one of the most successful poker training sites in the world,' WPN's website 'is considered the number one US poker site online,' and 'Poker News is the world's leading website dedicated to providing poker enthusiasts with a source of news, strategy, and more.' The Petitioner documented web traffic to these websites. He also submitted a letter from the former assistant director of a program stating the Petitioner 'has been an essential member' acting as educator and consultant to students, and a letter from a program coordinator stating the Petitioner's poker and chess courses were so popular there was a wait list and he was among the most highly rated instructors. A lecturer at a college submitted a letter stating the Petitioner gave a well-received speech at a Philosophy Club gathering.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence (citing Matter of Obaigbena). The documented web traffic permitted comparison between the three sites but did not show their standing in the field overall, which includes other poker sites. A reputation depends on outside perception, which cannot be established through statements from within the organizations. A printout from WPN's own website did not claim to be 'the number one US poker site'; rather it stated it was 'positioned to be an extremely important industry player in the future.' Regarding the institutional role, the Petitioner did not establish a critical role at the institutional level—the letters only described him as an essential member of a program, not critical to the institution as a whole, and the Petitioner did not establish that the Center or institution had distinguished reputations in their own right. For the college speech, the lecturer did not explain why the single event was critical to the college, and the Petitioner did not establish the college had a distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted a letter from the vice president of a Poker company stating that the Petitioner 'was called upon to judge the [founder's] work' and wrote an article on the site containing his evaluation of an 'Advanced Heads-Up Mastery Course' offered by the founder. The Petitioner described this article as a 'review.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director had concluded this criterion was met, but the AAO disagreed. The article was not an objective or independent evaluation of the course but was essentially an advertisement for a product offered by the Petitioner's employer, on the same website where the article appeared. Every opinion stated in the article was positive, and the piece ended with 'a free video from the course' and a coupon code 'for $150 off' the course. Authorship of this promotional piece did not amount to participation as a judge of the work of others.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted examples of columns he had written for online and print publications. The Petitioner initially contended these articles were comparable evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4). He also submitted web traffic statistics for some of the websites.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no need to invoke the comparable evidence clause. Contrary to the Director's conclusion that 'discussions of poker scenarios on poker websites' do not amount to scholarly articles, the AAO agreed with the Petitioner that 'scholarly articles' can encompass writings outside of academia. The articles appeared to qualify as scholarly because they were written in a technical manner presuming considerable knowledge of poker rules and terminology. However, the Petitioner did not establish that these articles appeared in major trade publications or other major media. The submitted web traffic statistics were raw numbers that did not provide a basis for comparison showing high circulation compared to other publications of their kind. Additionally, poker is not a profession as defined at INA § 101(a)(32), so a publication aimed at poker players is not a professional publication, notwithstanding the vernacular term 'professional poker players.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner asserted his work for WPN, Upswing Poker, and PokerNews included original contributions of major significance. He contended his 'innovative [contribution] provided to WPN stands as one of his most publicized contributions of major significance,' with publicity in the form of press releases and a blog post naming him as author. He submitted printouts of articles written for PokerNews. He noted he was writing a book (manuscript due November 2019, expected release June 2020). He submitted a letter from an editor-at-large of Ante Up Magazine stating the Petitioner's articles 'appear on websites considered to be the premiere [sic] online content source' and have 'proven to be the most sought-after content on leading poker websites,' and that his analysis for WPN was 'recognized as revolutionary and covered by websites owned by CBS; ABC; FOX; NBC; and more.' The editor-at-large also asserted the Petitioner was actively promoting poker to the underrepresented female population and had caught the attention of the Women's Poker Association, which invited him to discuss his work. The Petitioner submitted a two-part YouTube video interview with a Women's Poker Association representative (116 views for part 1, 71 views for part 2). He also submitted a letter from the PokerNews strategy editor praising his contributions. Following an RFE, the Petitioner submitted a 2019 article from Casino.org and an email from the Casino reporter asking the Petitioner for comments.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Petitioners must establish contributions were both original AND of major significance. The publicity for the WPN contribution came only from press releases apparently commissioned by the Petitioner. However beneficial the change may have been to WPN and its players, it did not follow that the contribution was of major significance throughout the field. The PokerNews articles were not evidence of their own significance. The unpublished book's significance could not be assessed prematurely. Letters from employers and others credited the Petitioner with original contributions but did not adequately explain how they were of major significance—describing work and praising it in hyperbolic but general and unsubstantiated terms could not suffice. The editor-at-large's claims were unsupported empirical assertions without data, and the 'revolutionary' coverage derived entirely from commissioned press releases with disclaimers on network affiliate sites (owned by Frankly, not the networks). The record did not document any significant controversy or discussion of the issue prior to the Petitioner's work, nor establish the extent to which other sites adopted his approach. WPN's CEO only claimed a positive impact on 'WPN and players alike,' not widespread adoption. The YouTube video view counts (116 and 71) did not indicate major significance. The Women's Poker Association itself had limited evidence of significance (founding article had only one comment). The Casino.org article appeared several months after the filing date and could not establish eligibility at time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1); moreover, the article merely quoted the Petitioner and did not identify his contributions or attest to their significance.

outcome_reasoning: The Petitioner did not submit required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents meeting at least three of the ten criteria, so no final merits determination under Kazarian was required. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the Petitioner established the acclaim and recognition required. The Petitioner sought a highly restrictive classification intended for individuals already at the top of their field, not those progressing toward the top. While the Petitioner established his work was of value to various publishers and that he taught a popular course, he did not establish sustained national or international acclaim or a 'career of acclaimed work.' Some evidence did not withstand scrutiny, such as authorship of what amounted to an advertisement formatted as a review and what appeared to be commissioned press releases creating the appearance of widespread publicity.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified what appeared to be the commissioning of press releases through 'Marketers Media' to create the appearance of widespread publicity across major television network affiliate websites, when the content was actually third-party promotional material with disclaimers disclaiming network responsibility. The AAO also found that the Petitioner's self-described 'review' article was actually an advertisement for his employer's product, ending with a promotional coupon code—characterized as 'an advertisement formatted as a review.' The AAO treated criterion (vii) (artistic exhibitions) as abandoned because the Petitioner did not contest the Director's conclusion on appeal, citing Matter of R-A-M-. The Casino.org article post-dated the filing and could not establish eligibility at time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1).
